coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of high luminescent Eu3+ doped nanoparticle and its application as highly sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ in real water and human blood serum
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Dwivedi, Arpita
Srivastava, Monika
Srivastava, Amit
Srivastava, S.K.
coredata.dc:description: The present work reports a highly efficient Ca doped Eu: Y2O3 i.e Ca0.05Eu0.01Y1.94O3 (CEY.) nanophosphor material synthesized through a facile combustion method, as a simple and selective turn-off fluorescence probe for the quantitative analysis of iron ions (Fe3+). The proposed sensor allows the quantification of iron in the range of 10 µM–90 µM with a limit of detection (LOD) ∼ 63.2 nM under the natural pH range. Moreover, CEY nanophosphor shows an excellent fluorescence phenomenon with a gradual increase in the Fe3+ ion concentration. It has been observed that the corresponding PL intensity gets completely quenched with 500 µM Fe3+ ion concentration. Furthermore, the applicability of the sensor as an efficient probe has been investigated with real water samples, iron tablets, and human blood serum (HBS). The selectivity of the probe has also been analyzed with various metal ions and biomolecules. Thus, in turn, the as-obtained sensing probe illustrates an excellent accuracy, sensitivity, and selectivity, and offers potential application in clinical diagnosis, biological and real water sample studies, with the detection of Fe3+ ion. Furthermore, it does not require any acidic medium for a level-free, and non-enzymic detection of a real sample with almost not affecting the sample quality and henceforth provides more reliable results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fe3+ ion detection
Ca-Eu: Y2O3
Turn-off
Fluorescence sensors
Human blood serum
Real water sample

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CEY nanophosphor and sensing solutions
Characterization of EY and CEY nanophosphor samples
UV–Vis and photoluminescence (PL) properties of EY and EYC nanophosphors
Selectivity for the proposed sensor for the Fe3+ ion
Sensing of Fe3+ ion and determination of performance parameters
Real sample analysis with CEY nano sensing probe
The sensing mechanism of Fe3+ ion with CEY

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the preparation of Ca-Eu: Y2O3 nanophosphor and quenching process involved with the addition of Fe3+ ion.
(a) XRD pattern (b) Magnified view of the reflection peak (2 2 2), of Eu: Y2O3 and Ca2+ co-doped Eu: Y2O3.

(a) TEM image, (b) particle size distribution, (c) SAED pattern and (d) HRTEM image, of as-synthesized Eu: Y2O3 (e) TEM image, (f) particle size distribution, (g) SAED pattern and (h) HRTEM image of Ca-Eu: Y2O3 nanophosphor.
FTIR spectra of the calcined (a) Eu: Y2O3 & (b) Ca-Eu: Y2O3 nanophosphor, and Raman spectra of (c) Eu: Y2O3 & (d) Eu: Y2O3 Co-doped with Ca nanophosphor.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra (b) optical energy bandgap (c) Photoluminescence excitation for emission monitoring at 612 nm and (d) emission spectra, of calcined Eu: Y2O3 and co-doped with Ca nanophosphor.
(a) Selectivity of Ca-Eu: Y2O3 against 10 µM of different metal ions and (b) selectivity of Ca-Eu: Y2O3 with different biomolecular reactive species of 10 µM concentration.

(a) Luminescence spectra of Ca-Eu: Y2O3 with different concentration of Fe3+ion (0–500 µM) inset show quenching of PL-intensity after addition of 10 µM and 500 µM Fe3+ ion solution (b) Stern-Volmer plot (c) linear fitting in the range 0–80 µM (d) Lineweaver-Burk plot of PL quenching with the addition of different concentration of Fe3+ ion.

(a) PL response of Ca-Eu: Y2O3 nanophosphor sample with the 10 µM and 50 µM concentration of Fe3+ ions in real samples (b) PL-spectra of Ca-Eu: Y2O3 nanophosphor with different concentration of human blood serum at room temperature (c) linear fitting of relative PL-intensity with concentration for Fe3+ detection in human blood serum for the range 5–30 µM.

(a) XRD pattern of Eu: Y2O3, Ca-Eu: Y2O3 and Ca-Eu: Y2O3 + Fe3+ ions (b) overlap of spectra between UV–vis absorption spectra of Fe3+ion in deionized water and emission spectra of Ca-Eu: Y2O3 nano-phosphor sample.
Estimated average crystallite size and lattice strain of as-synthesized Eu: Y2O3 and Ca-Eu: Y2O3 nanophosphors.
Raman lines of Eu: Y2O3 and Ca-Eu: Y2O3 nanophosphors.
Analytical results for the tests of addition/ recovery of Fe3+ ions in different real water samples and HBS samples.

entry.bold: Detected Fe3+ion with the probe CEY (µM)

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: *n = number of time measurements have been performed.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of high luminescent Eu3+ doped nanoparticle and its application as highly sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ in real water and human blood serum

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Institute of Science, Banaras Hindu University, Varanasi 221005, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Institute of Science, Banaras Hindu University, Varanasi 221005, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Technology, IIT (BHU), Varanasi 221005, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Technology, IIT (BHU), Varanasi 221005, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics TDPG College, VBS Purvanchal University, Jaunpur 222001, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics TDPG College, VBS Purvanchal University, Jaunpur 222001, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The present work reports a highly efficient Ca doped Eu: Y2O3 i.e Ca0.05Eu0.01Y1.94O3 (CEY.) nanophosphor material synthesized through a facile combustion method, as a simple and selective turn-off fluorescence probe for the quantitative analysis of iron ions (Fe3+). The proposed sensor allows the quantification of iron in the range of 10 µM–90 µM with a limit of detection (LOD) ∼ 63.2 nM under the natural pH range. Moreover, CEY nanophosphor shows an excellent fluorescence phenomenon with a gradual increase in the Fe3+ ion concentration. It has been observed that the corresponding PL intensity gets completely quenched with 500 µM Fe3+ ion concentration. Furthermore, the applicability of the sensor as an efficient probe has been investigated with real water samples, iron tablets, and human blood serum (HBS). The selectivity of the probe has also been analyzed with various metal ions and biomolecules. Thus, in turn, the as-obtained sensing probe illustrates an excellent accuracy, sensitivity, and selectivity, and offers potential application in clinical diagnosis, biological and real water sample studies, with the detection of Fe3+ ion. Furthermore, it does not require any acidic medium for a level-free, and non-enzymic detection of a real sample with almost not affecting the sample quality and henceforth provides more reliable results.

ce:section.ce:para: Metal ion detection has received the intriguing attraction of the researchers and opens a new avenue for the biomedical fields [1]. Among various metal ions (Cu2+, Fe3+, As3+, As5+, Hg2+), iron (Fe3+/Fe2+) plays an active role in our daily life, which mostly exists in the Fe3+ and Fe2+ forms in the various living organism [2,3]. Fe3+ and Fe2+ both, perform incredible roles in many biological and chemical processes involved in the human body such as energy metabolism, oxygen storage & transport, electron transfer reactions, mitochondrial respiratory chain, and many others [3]. The abnormal level of Fe3+ in the body can cause various serious diseases, like cancer, kidney diseases, cardiovascular diseases, Alzheimer’s, anemia, hepatitis Parkinson, and inhabitation of growth at a young age [4,5]. Subsequently, a trace level of iron plays a vital role in the health of the living organism and thus increase/decrease in the levels of iron not only affect humans but also disturb the ecological system. Therefore the evaluation of Fe3+ ions concentration is very important in drinking water quality as well as in human blood serum [3,6]. As per the norm of the World Health Organization (WHO), the threshold level of Fe3+ ion in drinking water is 5.36 × 10−6 M (0.3 ppm) and 10.74 µM/L to 30.43 µM/L in human blood serum [7,8]. Therefore, considering human health and the environment, it is imperative to develop a quick, easy, and sensitive approach for the detection of Fe3+ ions in a real water sample and human blood serum. Many methods have been reported till now for the determination of Fe3+ ions concentration. However, various limitations have been associated with these methods such as sophisticated handling, time consumption, use of various reagents, complex sample preparation protocols, on-time detection.

In recent years, photoluminescence-based sensing probe has fascinated more due to their high selectivity, sensitivity, clear imaging, and fast response. Lanthanides are considered to be the next generations green fluorescence nanomaterials and may be used as an alternative for conventional fluorescence probes for various applications [3,9]. These nanomaterials have a potential advantage due to high water dispersity, good biocompatibility, eco-friendly, flexibility with surface modification, resistance to photobleaching, etc. [2,10]. Therefore, rare-earth-based nanophosphors have been widely applied in diverse fields including bioimaging, nanomedicines, fluorescent sensing probe, solar cells, LED, fluorescent ink and optoelectronic devices, etc. [11,12].

Several techniques, based on rare earth photoluminescent nanomaterials and rare-earth metal–organic frame (MOF) have been reported for the detection of various heavy metals with Fe3+ ions in real water and biologically important reactive species [13]. Yu et al. have reported Eu-MOF and Tb-MOF which sense Fe3+ ions through fluorescence mechanism in water [14,15]. Guo et al. have also reported the Eu-MOF with excellent luminescence properties as a sensitive and selective probe for detection of Fe3+ ion in water with a limit of detection 0.39 µM [16]. Attia and his workers have developed a nano-optical sensor based on the Eu–Norfloxacin complex for measuring the histidine level in human blood serum with a detection limit of 6.5 × 10−10 M/L [17].

Among various lanthanide ions, Eu3+ has shown its remarkable importance and emits red color in the visible region with UV light irradiation. It exhibits electric dipole transition corresponding to 5D0→7F2 which is highly sensitive to the environment. These properties help in sensing due to the changed emission intensity of Eu3+ with various biomolecules. The fluorescence intensity of Eu: Y2O3 nanophosphor can be enhanced by suitable co-doping with various elements as Li+ Na+, K+, Ca2+, etc., and in turn lead to potential applications in various fields [12,18]. However, limited research work has been reported for the detection of Fe3+ ions concentration in water and human blood serum using these co-doped nanophosphors materials. Therefore, assuming these nanomaterials, a suitable sensing probe for quick, sensitive, & selective detection of Fe3+ ions in water and human blood serum can be developed.

Herein, we have developed a modest “turn-off” photoluminescent sensing probe for fast, sensitive, and selective detection of Fe3+ ions using CEY nanophosphors. As shown in Scheme Fig. 1 , the CEY nanophosphors have been synthesized through a solution combustion approach. The as-synthesized CEY nanophosphor shows excellent fluorescence properties, optical stability, and biocompatibility. It has also been found that the fluorescence intensity gets quenched with the addition of different concentrations of the standard solution prepared with FeCl3 & deionized water and shows a commendable potential for the sensing of trace Fe3+ ions concentrations. The luminescence intensity of the proposed material has been quenched through the energy transfer process in the presence of Fe3+ ions [2,15]. However, having an excellent performance for sensing Fe3+ with a standard solution, it has further been investigated with a real water sample as well as human blood serum. The proposed assay provides accurate results in intra- and inter-laboratory validation and henceforth as-synthesized CEY nanophosphor may be used as a sensitive low-cost, accurate & biocompatible probe for fast detection of Fe3+ and other biosensing applications.

All the chemicals and reagents were of analytical grade and, used without further purification. Urea (NH2CONH2) was purchased from Molychem, whereas Europium oxide (Eu2O3), Yttrium oxide (Y2O3), calcium oxide (CaO), and nitric acid (HNO3) were procured from Merk. FeCl3 and its competitive metal ions Zn2+, Mg2+, Na+, K+, Co2+, Sn2+, Cu2+, As3+, As5+, Fe2+, Cd2+ salts, and glycine, D-glucose, creatinine, L-glutathione, L-ascorbic, L-cystine, H2O2, & uric acid were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. A river water sample, mineral water sample, and Deionised water were collected from CWC, Navanita Beverage (Bisleri Plant), and USIC (BHU), Varanasi- India, respectively. However, human blood serum samples were collected from Prakash pathology and Radiology Centre Varanasi, India.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CEY nanophosphor and sensing solutions
ce:section.ce:para:
The CEY (Ca0.05Eu0.01Y1.94O3) nanophosphor was successfully prepared by following the steps with a slight modification in the combustion method. The synthesis process may have involved the following reaction form as [19]
xCa (NO3)2·nH2O + yEu (NO3)3·6H2O +(2-z-x)[Y(NO3)3·6H2O] + NH2CONH2 (as fuel) → Ca x Y(2-y-x)EuyO3 + H2O + gaseous products
where n is the number of water molecules available according to the metal nitrate nature, x represents a percentage of Calcium and y refers to a percentage of Europium oxide.

Briefly, the Europium nitrate (Eu(NO3)3) and yttrium nitrate (Y(NO3)3) and calcium nitrate (Ca(NO3)2) solutions were prepared by dissolving respective oxides of Eu (0.021 g), Y (1.47 g), and Ca(0.019 g) separately in 2 mL nitric acid and further diluted with deionized water. As received solution of Eu (NO3)3, Y(NO3)3 and Ca (NO3)2 were mixed and stirrerred for 30 min at 40 °C. A solution of urea (NH2CONH2, 3 g) with deionized water was also prepared in a separate beaker. Further, all these solutions were mixed in a beaker with as-prepared urea solution under continuous stirring and heating for 10–15 min, a transparent milky white solution was thus obtained. This end solution was transferred to a crucible and put in the furnace at 650 °C for 20 min and led to a flappy white powder after combustion. The collected nanophosphor powder was washed five times with acetone & ethanol and dried in the air. It was further put in the furnace at 900 °C for 8 h for calcination to eliminate/ reduce most of the impurities due to the presence of functional groups (nitrate and –OH) and increases the intensity of Y-O vibration. This raises the crystallinity and purity of nanophosphor material samples. Thus, the calcination supports enhancing the photoluminescence emission intensity of the as-synthesized material, which is very vital for sensing applications. A similar procedure has been adopted for the synthesis of (Eu0.01Y1.99O3) nanophosphor. The as-received powder was kept in a capsule for further characterization and sensing investigations.

Further, in a typical process, different concentrations of Fe3+ (0–500 µM) solutions were prepared from the standard stock solution of Fe3+ (5 × 10−3M). The standard stock solution was prepared by dissolving the 0.811 mg of FeCl3 in 10 mL of deionized water. The lower concentration of Fe3+solutions was obtained by dilution of an appropriate amount of stock solution in deionized water. All the solutions were prepared and used freshly, there was no precipitation of Fe (OH)3 with real water samples (river water, Mineral, water tap water, and iron tablets). For measurement purposes, different concentration of Fe3+ solution was added in the real samples.

To analyze the Fe3+ ion concentration in human blood serum, deproteinized serum samples of different concentrations were collected from Prakash Pathology and Radiology Centre, Varanasi. India. The collected sample was diluted 1000-fold with Tris-HCl 1 M of pH 7.0 and used without any treatments [20]. Further, the standard addition method was employed for sensing Fe3+ in human blood serum samples and spiked with the standard Fe3+ at different concentration levels (10–100 µM).
Moreover, the synthesis process of Tris-HCl has been discussed in the supplementary section.

The powder diffraction pattern of the as-synthesized sample was recorded through X-ray powder diffraction (XRD MiniFlax 600 diffractometer) with CuKα radiation (λ = 1.54046 Å). The XRD patterns were assessed in the 2θ range 10°–80° with step size 0.02°. The morphologies and microstructures of the synthesized material were studied by transmission electron microscope (TEM; TECHNAI G2 20, operated at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV). Perkin Elmer Lambda 750 UV–vis NIR (ultraviolet–visible near-infrared) spectrometer was used to access the UV–vis absorption spectra. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) of the samples were recorded by a BRUKER-ALPHA II FT-IR spectrometer at room temperature. The room temperature vibrational characteristics of the samples were carried out with a 532 nm Nd: YAG laser excitation using a micro- Raman spectrometer (Renishaw INVIA Raman Microscope) in a wavenumber range of 100–800 cm−1, while Photoluminescence characteristics of as-synthesized samples were estimated using Photoluminescence spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer, USA) system in the range 450–660 nm with step size 1 nm. The decay curves of the samples were measured with Fluorolog-3 (Horiba Jobin Yvon) spectrofluorometer excited with PMT attached 25 W pulsed Xenon lamp in phosphorescence mode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of EY and CEY nanophosphor samples
ce:section.ce:para:
The phase composition of the end product has been initially examined by XRD. Fig. 2 (a) shows the XRD pattern of Eu: Y2O3 (EY) and CEY nanoparticles. The XRD peaks of EY have been fully indexed to the cubic Y2O3 (with JCPDS: 65–3178) indicating that the Eu3+ ions have been doped into the Y2O3 lattice with no other impurity phase. It can be seen from Fig. 2(b), there is a little shift in the diffraction peak intensity towards a higher 2θ value due to co-doping with a larger ion of Ca2+ (ionic radius ∼ 100 pm) as compared to the Y3+ ions (ionic radius ∼ 90 pm) [21]. The peaks at 2θ ∼ 20.5°, 29.2 °, 33.8 °, 48.5 °, 57.8 ° correspond to the body center cubic (bcc) structure of CEY relative to the reflection planes (2 1 1), (2 2 2), (4 0 0), (4 4 0) and (6 2 2), respectively. The crystallite size has been deduced from Debye Scherrer's formula by opting for the (h00) peak,
(1)
D
=
0.9 λ
β
h
k
l
c
o
s
θ

Here, λ represents the wavelength of the x-rays employed (0.15418 nm), βhkl corresponds to the full width at half maximum (FWHM), and θ represents the angle of diffraction. The crystallite size along (h00) i.e., (4 0 0) and most intense peak (2 2 2) for the Eu: Y2O3 and Ca-Eu: Y2O3 have been found as ∼ 25.9 nm, ∼25.6 nm, and ∼ 27.2 nm, ∼27.3 nm, respectively. The average crystallite sizes of the as-synthesized samples have been given in Table 1 and predict that the crystallite size increases with Ca2+doping [22].

The average crystallite size of nanophosphor samples calculated with the D-S formula neglects the microstrain (ε) in the crystal. Further, the microstrain (ε) in the prepared nanophosphor sample has been assessed by employing the W-H Eq. (2) and represented in Fig. 1S (a & b). It is based on the principle of the approximate formulae for β, considered as the sum of the width due to the particle size βD and microstrain βε, (
β
=
β
D
+
β
ε
) [23].
(2)
β
D
C
o
s
θ
=
K
λ
D
+ 4
ε
S
i
n
(
θ
)
where β corresponds to the broadening (FWHM) of the XRD diffraction peak, θ is the angle of diffraction, K (=0.9) refers to the shape factor, λ represents the wavelength (0.15418 nm), D defines the average crystallite size of the particles and ε represents the strain associated with the nanophosphor particles. It has been observed that the strain present in the EY sample is less as compared to that of the CEY sample. This indicates that the surface atoms in the case of the Ca-doped sample have been increased as compared to the EY sample, and leads to the shift in the XRD peak as can also be seen in Fig. 2(b). The average crystallite size and lattice strain of the nanophosphor samples using Scherrer's formula and W-H plot have been presented in Table 1.

Furthermore, the homogeneity and distribution of the as-synthesized nanophosphor samples have been investigated through TEM studies. Fig. 3 (a-h) shows the TEM, HRTEM, and SAED micrographs of the as-synthesized EY and CEY nanophosphors. The typical TEM image of the solid sample indicates the homogeneous distribution of the spherical nanoparticles over the surface. It also indicates an agglomeration in both samples. The crystallite size of the EY nanophosphor lies in the range of 20–100 nm (Fig. 3(b)) with an average particle size of ∼ 48 nm. For CEY it ranges between is in 1–100 nm (Fig. 3(f)) with an average particle size of ∼ 45 nm. However, the reduction of particle size may be due to the reduction of oxygen bonds between the Y3+/Eu3+ and O by co-doping of Ca and results in agglomeration too (Fig. 3(e)) [24]. SAED pattern also reveals a crystalline nature of the nanophosphor samples having reflection planes as (2 2 2), (4 0 0), (4 4 0), (6 2 2) corresponding to EY and (2 1 1), (2 2 2), (4 0 0) for the CEY [25].

HRTEM image illustrates the lattice fringes with high crystallinity and predicts the interplanar spacing as ∼ 0.306 nm corresponding to the (2 2 2) plane of EY nanophosphor. Similarly, the interplanar spacing of CEY has also been estimated as ∼ 0.43 nm corresponding to the (2 1 1) plane. These results confirm that the Ca co-exists with EY in the CEY nanophosphor [11].

To get an insight into the attached functional groups with EY and CEY, the FTIR of the as-synthesized samples have been reordered in the range 500 to 3000 cm−1 as shown in Fig. 4 (a & b). The occurrence of an intense absorption band at 562 cm−1 corresponds to metal–oxygen (Y-O) stretching lattice vibration [26]. The band at 1500 cm−1 owes to –OH stretching vibrations in water molecules (–OH). The band near ∼ 1395 cm−1 and 1400 cm−1 owe to the C = O bonding vibration and bond NO3 – vibrations, respectively. It is evident from Fig. 4 (a) that the positions of the peaks do not get altered while the intensity gets increased slightly with the addition of Ca ions [27].

Fig. 4 (c & d) displays the Raman spectra of EY and CEY nanophosphors in the spectral range 50–700 cm−1. The major peak at 194 cm−1 attributes to Eg + Fg vibrations and the high intensity of the peak owes to large polarizability variation [28]. The peak at 375 cm−1 is the characteristic of the cubic structure of Y2O3 [29]. The appearance of a weak peak at 375 cm−1 owes to the breaking of translational symmetry of the crystal by Eu3+ doping. Furthermore, some additional Raman peaks at 50, 500, 630, and 670 cm−1 have also been observed in the EY and CEY nanophosphor samples attributing to the luminescence of Eu3+ ions occupied at symmetry sites with positions C3i. As the energy levels of Eu3+ ions occupying at C3i site are quite higher than that for C2 site, leads to the energy transfer to the C2 site and henceforth luminescence arises. Moreover, nanoscale dimension and incorporation of Eu3+ in the samples may also lead to the band broadening & inhomogeneous strains, which affects the position of Raman lines [30]. Further doping with Ca2+ also regulates the Raman band which can be confirmed through the disappearance of some bands, some have very low intensity, and some exhibit shift due to the change in the environment around Eu3+as shown in Fig. 4(d). However, the increase in the intensity of the Raman peaks has also been observed with no change in the cubic structure of the CEY nanophosphor. The Raman vibrational bands and spectral positions for EY and CEY nanophosphors have been presented in Table 2 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–Vis and photoluminescence (PL) properties of EY and EYC nanophosphors
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–Vis absorption spectra of the EY and CEY nanophosphor samples have been displayed in Fig. 5 (a). It shows the presence of a sharp absorption peak at 270 nm for EY nanophosphor and gets broaden with Ca2+ ions incorporation [31]. The optical band gap has been estimated from absorption spectra by following the Wood & Tauc formula and represented in Fig. 5(b) [32]. The optical energy band gap of EY and CEY nanophosphors has been deduced as 5.61 eV and 5.32 eV, respectively. It infers that the energy bandgap reduces with Ca2+ doping. Actually Ca2+ has been intrenched in Y2O3 lattice and produces oxygen vacancies, which in turn reduces the bandgap of nanophosphor [33,34].

Fig. 5 (c) reveals a similar trend for the PL excitation spectra for EY and CEY nanophosphors with 612 nm [27]. The Broadband at 237 nm appears due to charge transfer (CTB) from 2p orbit of O2– to 4f orbit of Eu3+ ion. The appearance of this peak indicates the increased crystallinity of the host lattice sample and homogenous distribution of Ca-Eu3+ in host lattice Y2O3. Additionally, some other sharp weak peaks (low energy) have also appeared at 360 nm (7F0→5D4), 378 nm (7F0→5L7), 394 nm (7F0→5L6), 414 nm (7F1→5D3), and 464 nm (7F0→5D2), respectively as shown in the inset of Fig. 5(c), due to 4f-4f transitions of Eu3+ ions [32,35].

Fig. 5(d) represents the emission spectra for as-synthesized EY and CEY nanophosphors. The various emission peaks between 550 and 700 nm have been recorded with an excitation wavelength of 394 nm. The presence of these emission peaks owes to the transition from excited state 5D0 to 7F j (j = 0–6) levels of Eu3+ ions. Host lattice (Y2O3) has two different sites for occupancy of the Eu3+ ions i.e., symmetric C2 and anti-symmetric C3i / S6 sites. The origin of (electric and magnetic dipole) transition of Eu3+ ions in the Y2O3 lattice from emitting levels to termination levels, depends on these locations of ions, and the type of transition with appropriate selection rules [36]. The strongest peaks at 613 nm and a small peak at 632 nm correspond to the hypersensitive (5D0→7F2) forced electric dipole transition, which provides intense red emission of Eu3+ ions. A weak emission peak at 594 nm corresponds to 5D0→7F1 due to the magnetic dipole transition. Electric dipole transitions (613 nm) are dominant due to the location of Eu3+ at a low symmetry site (without an inversion center). As the ionic radii of donar atom and coordination geometry of Ca (∼100 pm) are comparable to those of Eu and Y (∼94.7 pm), suits well for the substitution Eu3+in Y2O3 lattice and influences the physical–chemical biological properties. Substitution of Ca2+ in EY nanophosphor increases the disorder and marks a positive change in entropy due to an increase in interatomic distance of Eu3+/O2– which in turn causes enchantment in PL emission intensity of Eu3+ as can be seen in Fig. 5(d) [12]. In another case, when Ca2+ ions incorporate in the Eu: Y2O3 lattice, Ca2+ ions substitute the Y3+ ions by creating oxygen vacancies due to neutralizing charge inequality in the crystal. Hence, when Ca2+ is incorporated with Eu: Y2O3, not only energy transfer from Ca-Y2O3 to Eu3+ but also oxygen vacancies (O′) to Eu3+ cause the enhancement of PL intensity [12].

The decay curves of EY and CEY nanophosphors have been recorded using wavelength λ
ex = 394 nm and λ
em = 612 nm and shown in Fig. 2S (a). The photoluminescence lifetime (τ) for the most intense peak 5D0→7F2 has been recorded and fitted with the single exponential decay function
(4)
I =
I 0
e x p
( -
t τ
)
where I0 represents the initial emission intensity and τ specifies the PL lifetime [27]. Fig. 2S (a) indicates a decrease in the lifetime of CEY nanophosphor in comparison to EY from 2.16 to 2.04 ms, due to the increase in the number of ions occupying the surface states leading to the concentration-quenching [37]. This PL lifetime decay result confirms the enhancement in the intensity of PL emission spectra. To evaluate nanophosphor performance on the color luminescent emission, the Commission International de L’Eclairage (CIE) chromaticity coordinates have been evaluated. In general, the color emitted by any sample can be represented by color coordinate (x, y) in color space [38]. The CIE chromaticity coordinates for EY and CEY nanophosphors excited by 394 nm have been represented in Fig. 2S (b). The color emitted by both the samples falls in the red region of the CIE plot, EY gives a red color, and CEY nanophosphor provides emission in red–orange color. The chromaticity coordinates (x, y) of EY and CEY have been measured as (0.66, 0.34) and (0.57, 0.44), respectively with excitation wavelength λex ∼ 394 nm.

To analyze the performance of the proposed sensing probe, the effect of pH and response time have been explored. As shown in Fig. 3S (a), the value of (I0-I)/ I (where I0 and I represent the fluorescence intensities of CEY sample before and after addition of metal ions) is not very significant in the pH range 4–10 and indicates PL intensity for Fe3+ ion detection to remain almost constant [39,40]. PL-intensity is found to be maximum at pH □7.0 for the (Ca0.05Eu0.01Y1.94O3) CEY nanophosphor hence, it has been selected for the detection of Fe3+ ions.

Further, to analyze the stability of the CEY probe, the PL intensity response of CEY has been monitored for 1–30 days and no substantial change has been observed, as can be seen from Fig. 3S (b). Furthermore, the effect of quenching on the PL intensity with time gas has been studied (Fig. 3S (c)). The PL-quenching phenomenon starts in 5 min with the addition of Fe3+ ions and does not change significantly for the next 30 min. Henceforth, this sensing method provides an effective and efficient way for the rapid detection of Fe3+ [40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity for the proposed sensor for the Fe3+ ion
ce:section.ce:para:
Sensitivity is an important parameter to detect the exclusivity of the sensing molecule in the presence of various interfering molecules. Under optimal pH (7.0) condition, a series of other metal ions and biologically reactive species such as Zn2+, Mg2+, Na+, K+, Co2+, Sn2+, Cu2+, As3+, As5+, Fe2+, Cd2+ and glycine, D-glucose, creatinine, L-glutathione, L-ascorbic acid, L-cystine, H2O2, uric acid (concentration of each metal ion and biomolecules was taken as 10 µM) have been selected to evaluate the specificity of the proposed method for the sensing of Fe3+. To investigate its specificity, Fe3+ ion, metal ions, and biomolecules solutions have been added to the CEY nanoprobe. The relative PL-quenching efficiency vs
(
I 0
- I )
I
(where I0 and I represent the fluorescence intensities of CEY sample before and after addition of metal ions) with different interferants have been recorded and calculated as shown in Fig. 6 (a & b) [2,41]. It can be observed that the PL intensity is strongly quenched by Fe3+ ion as compared to other interfering ions in water as well as in human blood serum. The selectivity of nanoprobe towards Fe3+ over the other can be understood as the introduction of Ca- atom changes the electron density and leads to cause a change in the surface chemical activity of CEY. This produces effective absorption of the excitation energy of CEY by Fe3+ and there may be a comparative energy absorption process between these two, as given in Fig. 9 (b), and makes CEY more selective for Fe3+ [42].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of Fe3+ ion and determination of performance parameters
ce:section.ce:para:
The performance parameters such as sensitivity, detection range, and LOD of the proposed nanoprobe have been calculated with the help of the PL-quenching experiment and shown in Fig. 7 . Fig. 7(a) depicts the fluorescence quenching behavior of the proposed nanoprobe in presence of Fe3+ ion concentration with CEY. Fig. 7(c) & (d) depict the strong fluorescence intensity of CEY. We have taken a standard solution of different concentrations of Fe3+ ion and recorded the respective change in fluorescence response in the range of 0–500 µM concentration. The fluorescence intensity gradually decreases with increasing the Fe3+ ion concentration and starts quenching above 150 µM concentration and gets completely quenched at 500 µM. Fig. 7(b) represents a plot between intensity ratio (before and after quenching) with a concentration of quencher (Fe3+). It can be seen that the probe possesses good linearity between 0 and 90 µM of Fe3+ ion concentration range and turns upward further. This trend is basically due to static, dynamic quenching, and the energy transfer between Fe3+ and CEY sample [6,16]. The static quenching may occur due to the formation of complexes between Fe3+ions and the CEY at the ground state or coordination of Fe3+ ions and Oxygen atoms of CEY. However, the dynamic quenching process is governed by the collision between the electron-deficient Fe3+ ions and CEY [43]. To understand the static and dynamic quenching processes involved, the Stan-Volmer equation (S-V) and Lineweaver-Burk equation (L-B) have been plotted with the Eq. (5) and (b) [10,16,44]:
(5)
I 0
I
= 1 +
K
s v
M
(6)
1
I -
I 0
=
1
I 0
+
K m
I 0
C q

Here I0 and I represent the intensity in the absence and presence of Fe3+ ions, respectively, [M] is the molar concentration of Fe3+, Ksv and Km are the Stan-Volmer constant [M−1] Lineweaver-Burk quenching constant [M], respectively. The S-V plot has been used to determine the quenching efficiency. Fig. 7(c) depicts that the S-V plot possesses an approximately linear range of 10–90 µM of Fe3+ ion concentration. The correlation coefficient (R2) and Ksv value are calculated and found 0.999, 8.333x104 M−1, respectively for the suitable concentrations of Fe3+ ion. Further, with the help of an S-V plot the limit of detection (LOD) for Fe3+ detection has been calculated as 63.2 nM according to the formula LOD = 3σ/S, where σ represents the standard deviation and S defines the slope of the linear calibration plot associated with the number of repetition n as 3 (Fig. 7 c) [10,40,45]. The as-obtained result is found to be better than the previously reported work for the detection of Fe3+. A comparison of various performance parameters with other reported work has been summarized in Table 3S [10,16,39–41,45].

Furthermore, to get insight into the kinetics of fluorescence quenching, a graph between 1/(I-I0) and concentration [M] has been plotted in concurrence with the “double reciprocal plot” or “Lineweaver-Burk plot” as given by Eq. (6) in Fig. 7(d) and the Correlation coefficient (R2) and Km values have been found as 0.999 and 199 µM for Fe3+ ion concentration. The value of Km (199 µM) infers CEY nanophosphor has a great affinity towards complex formation with Fe3+ ion. A little amount of CEY is required to attain the given rate and thus the lower concentration of Fe3+ solution can also sense through the proposed sensing probe [46].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real sample analysis with CEY nano sensing probe
ce:section.ce:para:
Further, to confirm the accuracy and application of the proposed probe with the real water sample and HBS, the quenching experiment has been carried out with the CEY nanoprobe. The different real samples have been collected from- river water (Rivers Ganga and Varuna at Varanasi, India), mineral water, tap water, and iron tablets. All the collected water samples have been first filtered through cellular filter paper before sensing and maintained the pH at 7. In all the collected samples, different amounts of standard samples of Fe3+ (10 µM and 50 µM) have been added and recorded the fluorescence response. A similar type of quenching phenomenon with these samples has been obtained with the standard solutions as shown in Fig. 8
(a). It has also been found that the real sample response quite close to the added Fe3+ ions as can be seen from Table 3tbl3; The result is very encouraging since the sample recoveries lie in the range of 95%-105% having a relative error of less than 5.41% with n = 3.

The practical application of the deigned sensing probe has also been carried out with human blood serum. For the quantification of the concentration of Fe3+ in human blood serum, a standard addition method using FeCl3 solution was carried out, and a respective change in PL-response has been recorded [20]. The fluorescence intensity decreases with increasing concentration Fe3+ ions in the HBS sample as can be seen in Fig. 8(b) and possesses a linear relationship with volume in the range 10–30 µM having a correlation coefficient of 0.998 (Fig. 8(c)). These results indicate that the method employed is capable of quantifying the Fe3+ in human blood serum quite effectively. The recovery of the added samples has been found in the 96–105% range with a relative error of less than 4.15% (n = 3) as given in Table 3 . These results highlight the reliability, capability, accuracy, convenience of the method in detecting the concentration of Fe3+ in biological and environmental samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The sensing mechanism of Fe3+ ion with CEY
ce:section.ce:para: The sensing mechanism of the proposed nanoprobe for the detection of Fe3+ ions has been represented in Fig. 9. Usually, there may be various possible and plausible reasons for the fluorescence quenching, particularly the change in the structure of the as-synthesized sample due to the addition of Fe3+ ions, energy transfer, or charge transfer. In order to investigate structural change with the addition of Fe3+ ion in the CEY nanophosphor sample, the XRD has been carried out and the results have been presented in Fig. 9(a). The given patterns indicate that the fluorescence quenching is not instigated by the collapse of a structural framework.

Further, it is well known that the energy transfer process plays an important role in the fluorescence quenching mechanism [2,10]. Therefore, the quenching process caused by the gradual addition of Fe3+ ions may elucidate the energy transfer mechanism. Fluorescence in CEY nanophosphor occurs due to non-radiative energy transfer from Y2O3 to Eu3+ ions. Co-doping with the Ca2+ ions leads to enhance the energy transfer process by increasing the population of Eu3+ ions which intern enhances the PL intensity. Basically, the substitution of Ca2+ in EY leads to the disorder by affecting the interatomic distance of Eu3+/O2−and creates oxygen vacancies for neutralizing the charge inequality created in the crystal which in turn enhances the charge density of Eu3+ ions, making it more available for the energy transfer processes. Additionally, the oxygen vacancies created due to doping also help in energy transfer to Eu3+ and thereby enhancement in PL intensity [42]. Moreover, with the addition of the addition of Fe3+ on CEY, it starts interacting with Eu-O bonds, leads to partial interruption of electron density of Eu3+–O2– atoms, and thus the effective electron transfer between the radiative center and host gets decreased. In another process, Fe3+ interacts with oxygen vacancies created due to Ca2+ doping and inhibit energy transfer which might be a reason for the decrease in the PL emission intensity [12,47] as given in Fig. 4S. Hence, interaction with Fe3+ ion on CEY leads to the quenching of fluorescence. To verify our conjecture, the absorption spectra (UV–Vis) of Fe3+ solution in deionized water and excitation spectra of CEY have been recorded & represented in Fig. 9(b). It shows that Fe3+ can effectively absorb the excitation energy of CEY and restrict the fluorescence of the Eu3+ ions. Therefore Fe3+ ions may efficiently accept the energy of exciting light, which inhibits the energy transfer efficiency from CEY to Eu3+ ions and results in quenching of PL emission shown in Fig. 5(d) [2].

In summary, the red–orange light-emitting nanophosphor i.e., CEY has been successfully synthesized by adopting the combustion method with a slight modification. Various characterization techniques confirm the cubic structure of the as-synthesized nanomaterial. The PL spectra reveal that the electric dipole transitions (7F2) are more dominating and the emission intensity of CEY gets increased by fivefold to that of EYN with Ca doping. The extraordinary luminescence properties establish the fact that as-synthesized CEY may be applied as a selective and sensitive probe for the detection of Fe3+ions in real samples. The proposed probe based on turnoff- fluorescence mechanism exhibits a low limit of detection (63.2 nM) with a linear range of 10–90 µM under optimized conditions (pH 4–10) and does not get affected by the interference of various other metal/biological reactive species. Henceforth, the proposed sensing probe displaying significant results with the real water sample, iron tablet, and human blood serum, can be streamlined in medical, pharmaceuticals, and industries for the measurement and analysis of iron concentration in the wastewater.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge Prakash Pathology and Radiology Centre-Varanasi, India for providing human blood serum samples. One of the Authors (AD) also wishes to acknowledge University Grants Commission (UGC), India for providing a research fellowship. MS conveys her gratitude to the DST, New Delhi (SR/WOS-A/CS-52/2018) for the WOS fellowship.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Synthesis of Tris-HCl, W-H plot, lifetime decay and CIE diagram, Effect of pH, Comparison table. Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2021.119942.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
